target.title: [codex] fix devices approve local fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- rebase the device approval fix onto current `main` after the recent gateway-side pairing changes
- keep `devices approve` on the Gateway-first path so Gateway authorization, approval logging, and `device.pair.resolved` broadcasts are preserved
- disable local pairing fallback when explicit `--url`, `--token`, or `--password` flags are provided, with CLI tests and docs coverage

## Root cause / resolution
The original local-first approval change is no longer the right shape on current `main`: it could bypass Gateway approval side effects and device-token authorization checks. This update keeps approval routed through `device.pair.approve` first and only allows the existing local loopback fallback for implicit local credentials. When a caller provides explicit Gateway/auth flags, the CLI now stays on the Gateway-authenticated path instead of silently reading local pairing files.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Explicit `--token` auth on a local loopback `devices list` command must stay on the Gateway-authenticated path and must not silently fall back to local pairing files.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS source checkout on this rebased branch, with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and a current-branch loopback gateway started on `127.0.0.1:18891` using `pnpm openclaw gateway run --bind loopback --port 18891 --force --allow-unconfigured`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Started the temporary gateway, waited for the port to bind, then ran `pnpm openclaw devices list --json --token [REDACTED_TOKEN]` with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=18891` and the same temporary config/state environment.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output and gateway log excerpt from the live local run:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw devices list --json --token [REDACTED_TOKEN]
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)
[openclaw] Could not start the CLI.
[openclaw] Reason: gateway closed (1008): unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18891
Source: local loopback
Config: [TMPDIR]/openclaw.json
Bind: loopback

$ tail -n 30 gateway.log
[gateway] http server listening (9 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 3.3s)
[gateway] ready
[ws] unauthorized ... client=cli cli v2026.5.8 role=operator scopes=1 auth=token device=yes ... reason=token_mismatch
[ws] closed before connect ... code=1008 reason=connect failed
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The explicit-token CLI call failed through Gateway auth with `token_mismatch` on the local loopback gateway. The output did not include `Direct scope access failed; using local fallback.`, and the gateway log recorded `auth=token`, which shows the command remained on the explicit Gateway auth path.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Tests
- `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/devices.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: Preserve Open WebUI UUID sessions across Control UI
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve Open WebUI UUID chat ids instead of canonicalizing them to `agent:main:<uuid>` in the gateway session store
- normalize `agent:main:<uuid>` back to the original bare UUID when the Control UI reads or writes chat URLs
- pass agent context into transcript lookup so agent-scoped histories resolve the correct transcript file
- add regression coverage for UUID preservation, transcript fallback, and Control UI URL normalization

## Problem
Open WebUI uses opaque UUIDs for chat ids. When the same chat is opened through the OpenClaw Control UI, OpenClaw currently treats that UUID like an ordinary bare session key and canonicalizes it into an agent-prefixed key.

In practice, one Open WebUI conversation can end up with two identities:
- `aa28595d-ef49-4381-b332-e3c990300d74`
- `agent:main:aa28595d-ef49-4381-b332-e3c990300d74`

Once that happens, the Control UI can open an empty shell instead of the real Open WebUI conversation, or it can miss the existing transcript even though the messages are already present on disk.

This is a narrow integration bug rather than a broad session cleanup issue. It shows up when Open WebUI is used as a multi-chat frontend for OpenClaw and the same UUID-backed chat is later opened in the Control UI.

## Why this matters
Open WebUI is a useful ChatGPT-style frontend for OpenClaw because it makes it easy to keep multiple long-running topic-specific chats in the browser. Without this fix, that workflow still requires a downstream monkey patch after upgrades, because the shared Open WebUI/OpenClaw session identity drifts apart.

## What changed
1. Gateway session-key resolution now preserves opaque bare UUIDs instead of rewriting them to agent-scoped keys.
2. The Control UI normalizes `agent:main:<uuid>` URLs back to the original bare UUID and keeps that normalized form in the address bar.
3. Session history loading now carries `agentId` through transcript lookup so agent-scoped stores resolve the right transcript path for these chats.
4. Regression tests cover the gateway store key, transcript fallback, and Control UI URL behavior.

## Scope
This PR intentionally stays focused on shared session identity and transcript lookup for Open WebUI-backed chats.

It does not try to mirror external turns into Open WebUI's own SQLite history database. That visual-sync behavior is a separate follow-up concern.

## Testing
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/session-utils.fs.test.ts -t "uses agentId fallback to read agent-scoped transcripts from OPENCLAW_HOME"`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "preserves opaque bare UUID session keys"`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.test.ts`
- manual verification against a deployed Open WebUI + OpenClaw setup on `v2026.3.24`: opening the same UUID-backed chat in the Control UI reused the existing history instead of splitting into a second session

Fixes #49202

target.title: fix(gateway): bind loopback MCP scope to per-backend bearer token (#64993)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes loopback MCP scope spoofing (issue #64993, CWE-285/639/807/345, OWASP A01:2021).

The loopback MCP HTTP server (`/mcp`) authenticated each request with a 32-byte bearer token but then read per-request **scope** (`sessionKey`, `accountId`, `messageChannel`, `senderIsOwner`) from mutable HTTP headers. The token was injected into spawned agent backend env, so any code executing inside a backend (realistically via prompt injection reaching a shell/HTTP tool) could reuse the token and set `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner: true` to receive the owner-only tool catalog and call owner-gated tools.

This change binds the authoritative scope to the per-backend token:

- **Server side** (`src/gateway/mcp-http.loopback-runtime.ts`, `mcp-http.request.ts`, `mcp-http.ts`): token table `{ token -> scope }` registered via `registerMcpLoopbackToken(scope)`. `validateMcpLoopbackRequest` does a constant-time lookup against all registered tokens and returns the authoritative scope. Scope HTTP headers are no longer read.
- **Client config** (`createMcpLoopbackServerConfig`): emits only `Authorization` and the non-security `x-openclaw-agent-id`; the four scope headers (`x-session-key`, `x-openclaw-account-id`, `x-openclaw-message-channel`, `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner`) are gone.
- **Backend spawn** (`src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts`): per-backend `registerMcpLoopbackToken({ sessionKey, accountId, messageProvider, senderIsOwner })` call after `ensureMcpLoopbackServer`. Returned token goes into `OPENCLAW_MCP_TOKEN`. The four scope env vars are removed. Deregistration is wired to backend lifecycle.
- **Cache** (`McpLoopbackToolCache`): gains `invalidateForScope(...)` called from `unregisterMcpLoopbackToken` so residual scoped catalogs cannot survive a revoked token.
- **Tests**: the previous positive assertion that `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner` headers produce different `resolveGatewayScopedTools` calls is inverted into a negative regression test. New tests cover token-identity scope isolation, unknown-token 401, token revocation cache invalidation, and the absence of scope headers from the generated client config.

Implements the maintainer-endorsed landing plan (vincentkoc, 2026-04-12): interim server-side scope pinning + structural token-bound scope + negative regression test, shipped together.

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm tsgo`
- [ ] `pnpm check`
- [ ] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts` — scoped, includes the new negative regression test
- [ ] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-runner/` — scoped, confirms `registerMcpLoopbackToken` wiring and env-var removal
- [ ] `pnpm test` — landing gate
- [ ] `pnpm build` — hard gate (touches module exposed via loopback config surface)
- [ ] Verify no stale references to `OPENCLAW_MCP_SESSION_KEY` / `_ACCOUNT_ID` / `_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` / `_SENDER_IS_OWNER` or the four scope headers remain in docs/scripts/bundled plugins

Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64993

target.title: feat(codex-cli): prefer output-last-message artifacts
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: direct `codex-cli` runs still finalize the user-visible reply from stdout even though Codex can write a dedicated final-message artifact
- Why it matters: stdout is useful for transport and telemetry, but it is a weaker source of truth for the final assistant answer than Codex's explicit `--output-last-message` output
- What changed: `codex-cli` runs now allocate a temp `--output-last-message` file, prefer that artifact when it contains text, and keep stdout parsing as the fallback path
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): other CLI backends still use their existing stdout/json/jsonl parsing paths, and this PR does not change failure classification or streaming behavior

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #65074
- Related #39317
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the direct `codex-cli` runner only treated stdout parsing as the final reply source even though Codex already exposes a more stable final-message artifact contract
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no runner-level test that forced stdout and the final-message artifact to disagree and verified that OpenClaw finalized from the artifact
- Contributing context (if known): Codex CLI already supports `--output-last-message`, but OpenClaw was not wiring that contract into the `codex-cli` backend path

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when Codex writes both stdout JSONL and an `--output-last-message` file, OpenClaw should prefer the artifact when it is non-empty and fall back to parsed stdout when the artifact is empty
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the exact runner boundary where args are built, the artifact is read, stdout is still parsed, and the final reply is selected
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none covered the artifact-vs-stdout precedence or Codex message-content JSONL fallback
- If no new test is added, why not:

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `codex-cli` replies now finalize from Codex's explicit final-message artifact when available, which makes delegated Codex runs less dependent on stdout formatting details.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[codex exec --json] -> [stdout parsed] -> [reply text selected from stdout]

After:
[codex exec --json --output-last-message <file>]
  -> [stdout parsed for session/usage/fallback]
  -> [artifact read]
  -> [artifact text wins when present]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This only adds Codex's own `--output-last-message` flag plus a temp file under OpenClaw's preferred temp dir. The file is per-run, read back locally, and deleted in cleanup even when parsing falls back to stdout.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm/vitest
- Model/provider: `codex-cli/gpt-5.4`
- Integration/channel (if any): direct CLI runner
- Relevant config (redacted): default `codex-cli` backend with `--json`

### Steps

1. Run a direct `codex-cli` execution through the CLI runner.
2. Have Codex emit stdout JSONL and write a final-message artifact.
3. Compare the final reply selection when the artifact is populated vs empty.

### Expected

- OpenClaw should prefer the artifact text when it exists and keep stdout parsing as the fallback.

### Actual

- Before this change, the runner always finalized from stdout because it never requested or read Codex's final-message artifact.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: targeted vitest run for `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`, and `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: non-empty artifact overriding stdout; empty artifact falling back to stdout; Codex JSONL message content still parsing to plain assistant text
- What you did **not** verify: full repo-wide `pnpm check` / `pnpm test` lanes and a live end-to-end Codex CLI run against the actual binary in this branch

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: temp artifact cleanup could leak files if the runner exits early
  - Mitigation: cleanup runs in the outer `finally`, and the unit test asserts the artifact path is removed after success
- Risk: Codex stdout fallback could regress if the artifact is empty and stdout uses the normal message-content JSONL shape
  - Mitigation: `parseCliJsonl` now extracts text from Codex message content records, and the fallback path is covered by a dedicated unit test

AI-assisted: Yes (Codex). Testing: targeted vitest coverage listed above. I attempted `codex review --base origin/main`, but the local Codex CLI is currently rate-limited.

target.title: refactor(gateway): unify config-derived cache invalidation on hot reload
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: module-scoped caches for model catalog, models.json fingerprints, and context windows use the same `let promise = null` pattern as process-lifetime code-module caches — but unlike code modules, they hold state derived from `openclaw.json` that must refresh on config changes. Hot reload missed them because they weren't recognized as a distinct category.
- **Solution**: introduce a lightweight registry (`config-derived-caches.ts`) that lets each config-derived cache declare which config prefixes it depends on. The reload handler calls `invalidateConfigDerivedCaches(changedPaths)` once — no per-cache if-statements needed.
- **Scope boundary**: process-lifetime caches (40+ lazy `import()` calls) and request-scoped caches (`loadConfig()` 200ms TTL) are NOT touched. Only the 3 caches that actually hold config-derived state are migrated.

## The three cache categories

| Category | Lifetime | Example | Invalidation | Touched? |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|----------|
| Process | Never | `import("./xxx.runtime.js")` | Not needed | No |
| **Config-derived** | **Config change** | **modelCatalog, contextWindow, modelsJson** | **Registry → hot reload** | **Yes** |
| Request | 200ms TTL | `loadConfig()` | Auto-expire | No |

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/agents/config-derived-caches.ts` | New — registry + `invalidateConfigDerivedCaches()` (~60 lines) |
| `src/agents/config-derived-caches.test.ts` | New — 8 tests covering prefix matching, exact match, no-match, catch-all, multi-prefix |
| `src/agents/model-catalog.ts` | +10 lines — register `modelCatalog` (prefixes: `["models"]`) |
| `src/agents/models-config.ts` | +9 lines — register `modelsJsonReady` (prefixes: `["models"]`) |
| `src/agents/context.ts` | +13 lines — register `contextWindowCache` (prefixes: `["models"]`) |
| `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts` | +6 lines — single `invalidateConfigDerivedCaches()` call after `resetDirectoryCache()` |

## Why this approach over per-cache if-statements

Three competing PRs (#49586, #49600, #49795) each add an if-statement for model catalog only. But context window cache and models.json fingerprint cache have the same stale-after-reload problem — they just haven't been reported yet. This PR fixes all three and ensures future config-derived caches only need a `registerConfigDerivedCache()` call; the reload handler needs no changes.

## Test plan

- [x] 8 new unit tests for registry (prefix matching, exact match, no-match, catch-all, multi-prefix, substring safety)
- [x] Existing `config-reload.test.ts` — 22 tests pass
- [x] Existing `model-catalog.test.ts` — passes
- [x] TypeScript check passes (no new errors introduced)
- [ ] CI

target.title: feat(feishu): extract option, options, form_value from card action callbacks
target.body:
## Problem

When users interact with Feishu interactive card components like `select_static` (dropdowns), `checkbox` (multi-select), or `form` (form submissions), the selected values are lost because `handleFeishuCardAction` only extracts data from `action.value`.

Feishu sends selection data in separate fields:
- `action.option` — for `select_static` single selection
- `action.options` — for `checkbox` / `multi_select` multiple selections  
- `action.form_value` — for `form` submission data

These fields are completely ignored by the current implementation, making it impossible for AI Agents to know what the user actually selected.

Related issue: #42754

## Solution

1. **Extended `FeishuCardActionEvent` type** to include `option`, `options`, and `form_value` fields
2. **Updated content extraction logic** to merge these fields with `action.value` when present, using priority: `form_value` > `options` > `option` > existing behavior
3. **Maintained full backward compatibility** — existing button interactions (`value.text`, `value.command`) work exactly as before

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/card-action.ts` — Type definition + extraction logic (14 lines changed)
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot.card-action.test.ts` — 4 new test cases (90 lines added)

## Test Plan

- [x] `select_static` with `option` field — value merged into JSON output
- [x] `checkbox` with `options` array — values merged into JSON output  
- [x] `form` with `form_value` object — values merged into JSON output
- [x] Existing button with `value.command` — backward compatible, unchanged behavior
- [x] All 6 tests pass (`vitest`)
- [x] TypeScript compilation passes

## AI Disclosure

This PR was authored with assistance from an AI coding assistant (Claude/Cursor). All code changes have been reviewed and tested.

target.title: fix(android): preserve websocket transport and reduce pairing churn
target.body:
## Summary

This PR started as the Android transport fix for gateway setup codes and manual gateway URLs that carried `wss://` / `ws://`, but were still being normalized into HTTP-facing URLs in actual transport flows.

Live testing over Tailscale Serve exposed a second Android UX issue after the transport path was fixed: once the phone could actually reach the gateway, the onboarding/connect UI kept auto-retrying on `pairing required`, which created repeated pending pairing requests and made approval racey.

This branch now fixes both issues.

## What changed

### 1. Preserve WebSocket transport URLs

- keep a distinct `transportUrl` alongside the human-facing `displayUrl`
- use `transportUrl` in Android onboarding/connect flows when dialing the gateway
- keep `displayUrl` as `http(s)://...` for presentation only
- preserve explicit non-default WebSocket ports in `transportUrl`
  - omit only standard defaults (`ws:80`, `wss:443`)
  - keep `:18789` for `ws://...:18789`

### 2. Reduce pairing-request churn

- stop looping auto-retries forever while the UI remains in the same `pairing required` state
- retry once when pairing first becomes required, instead of generating a new pending request every few seconds

## User-visible effect

Before:
- `wss://...` setup codes could be used as `https://...` during transport, causing failures like:
  - `Expected HTTP 101 response but was '200 OK'`
- after the route was corrected, Android could still create rapidly rotating pending pairing requests during onboarding

After:
- Android preserves the correct WebSocket transport URL
- `pairing required` means the route is working and approval is the next step
- the app no longer churns fresh pairing requests continuously while waiting for approval

## Validation

```bash
./gradlew :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest \
  --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.GatewayConfigResolverTest \
  --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.GatewayPairingRetryLogicTest \
  --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.OnboardingFlowLogicTest \
  :app:assemblePlayDebug
```

Live-tested against a gateway behind Tailscale Serve.

- Why it matters: repeated main-thread warnings are a signal of avoidable UI responsiveness risk in a frequently queried permission snapshot path.
- What changed: reused a single `CLLocationManager` instance, introduced a nonisolated speech-status accessor, and added tests around the updated permission snapshot behavior.

target.title: fix(agents): normalize mangled tool names and IDs from OpenAI-compati…
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Some OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. Kimi) send tool call IDs like `functions.exec:0` as `functions exec:0` (space instead of dot) and tool names like `exec` as `functions exec`. Tool result matching fails, causing "Tool functions exec not found" errors.
- **Why it matters:** Users on Kimi and similar providers cannot complete tool-using flows; matching is done by ID and name, and the mangled forms did not match.
- **What changed:** Added `normalizeMangledToolCallId()` in `tool-call-id.ts` to rewrite `functions ` -> `functions.` in IDs; use it in `extractToolResultId()` and as the key in `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist()` so mangled and canonical IDs map to the same sanitized value. Added `normalizePrefixedToolName()` in `session-transcript-repair.ts` to strip `functions.` / `functions ` from tool names; applied in assistant block handling, `hasToolCallName`, and `normalizeToolResultName()` so tool-result names are normalized and pairing works.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No change to provider clients or wire format; only transcript repair and ID sanitization for matching.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39091
- Related: normalizeToolResultName fix included

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Tool-using flows with OpenAI-compatible providers that mangle tool IDs/names (e.g. Kimi) now match tool results to tool calls correctly; "Tool functions exec not found" is resolved.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Provider: OpenAI-compatible (e.g. Kimi) that sends `functions exec:0` / `functions exec` style IDs and names

### Steps

1. Run agent with such a provider; trigger a tool call (e.g. exec).
2. Before fix: tool result may not match, "Tool functions exec not found" or similar.
3. After fix: IDs and names normalize; pairing and sanitization map mangled and canonical forms to the same value.

### Expected

Tool results pair with assistant tool calls; no spurious "tool not found" from ID/name mangling.

### Actual

As expected after the fix.

## Evidence

- `normalizeMangledToolCallId` and `extractToolResultId` / `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist` tests in `tool-call-id.test.ts`.
- `sanitizeToolUseResultPairing` test with mangled ID and prefixed name in `session-transcript-repair.test.ts`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Unit tests added; logic follows issue and Greptile feedback (compare original rawToolName in normalizeToolResultName, use normalized ID as map key).
- **Edge cases checked:** Empty string, already-canonical IDs, names without prefix.
- **What you did not verify:** Live Kimi or other provider E2E.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert the commit.
- Files: `src/agents/tool-call-id.ts`, `src/agents/session-transcript-repair.ts`.

## Risks and Mitigations

**None.** Normalization is additive and only affects matching/sanitization; no new execution paths.

target.title: fix(exec): auto-approve recognized read-only boolean flags on default safe bins
target.body:
## Summary

Default safe bins (`cut`, `head`, `tail`, `tr`, `uniq`, `wc`) exist to auto-approve **stdin-only text filters** without a manual approval prompt. But the short-option validator only had an *accept* path for **value-consuming** flags: `consumeShortOptionClusterToken` skipped any flag not in `allowedValueFlags` and, if the cluster contained no value flag, fell through to a terminal `return -1` (reject). So a cluster of pure **boolean** short flags was never auto-approved.

Net effect: the most common read-only invocations of these bins — `wc -l`, `wc -w`, `tr -d`, `tr -s`, `uniq -c` (and `sort -n`/`-r` for operators who add `sort`) — were force-routed to manual approval, even though the bins are stdin-only and every dangerous flag is already in `deniedFlags`. The presence of short flags in `deniedFlags` (`sort: -o, -T`; `jq: -L, -f`; `grep: -f, -r, -R`) shows the intended model is a denylist over recognized flags, so rejecting legitimate boolean flags is an oversight, not a deliberate narrowing.

## Fix

Add an `allowedBooleanFlags` allowlist to the safe-bin profile model and populate it for the six default bins with their read-only, no-side-effect boolean flags:

- `wc`: `-l -w -c -m -L` (+ long forms)
- `tr`: `-d -s -c -C -t`
- `uniq`: `-c -d -u -i -z`
- `cut`: `-s -n -z --complement --only-delimited`
- `head` / `tail`: `-q -v -z` (+ `--quiet/--silent/--verbose/--zero-terminated`). `tail -f`/`-F`/`--follow`/`--retry` are deliberately **excluded** so the auto-approve path can never stream/hang.

The validator now treats a recognized boolean flag as "no value, keep scanning" and accepts a cluster made entirely of recognized boolean flags, while an **unrecognized** short flag still hits `return -1` (fail-closed). The long-option path needs no logic change: feeding boolean long flags into `knownLongFlags` makes `consumeLongOptionToken`'s existing `if (!expectsValue) return index + 1` branch reachable for the first time. Denied flags are still checked first, so `sort -o`, `wc --files0-from`, etc. remain rejected.

This is intentionally **not** exposed as a new `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles` config field (root `AGENTS.md`: the config/env surface bar is high). It fixes the curated built-in default-bin profiles only; custom user profiles keep the existing `allowedValueFlags` / `deniedFlags` model. `sort`/`grep`/`jq` are not default safe bins and are left for a follow-up.

## Behavior change

| Invocation | before | after |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `wc -l`, `wc -w`, `wc --lines` | needs approval | **auto-approved** |
| `tr -d abc`, `tr -s ' '` | needs approval | **auto-approved** |
| `uniq -c`, `cut -s -f 1` | needs approval | **auto-approved** |
| `tr -S a b` (unknown short flag) | needs approval | needs approval (unchanged) |
| `sort -o out.txt` (denied) | needs approval | needs approval (unchanged) |
| `wc --files0-from=list` (denied) | needs approval | needs approval (unchanged) |

## Diff size

```
 docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md       |  4 +-
 src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts  | 32 +++++++++++++
 src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts  | 73 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++-
 src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-validator.ts | 23 +++++----
 src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts      | 30 ++++++++++++
 5 files changed, 152 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)
```

## Test plan

- [x] New validator unit tests (`exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts`): boolean short flags accepted (`wc -l`, `-lw`, `uniq -c`, `tr -d`), boolean long flags + abbreviations (`wc --lines`, `--word`), value-on-boolean rejected (`wc --lines=5`), unknown short flags rejected (`tr -S`, `wc -Z`), mixed boolean+value cluster preserved (`uniq -cf 2`).
- [x] New end-to-end-gate tests (`exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts`) through `isSafeBinUsage`: `wc -l`, `wc --words`, `uniq -c`, `tr -d abc` auto-allowed; the existing `tr -S a b` -> rejected guard kept.
- [x] Full `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` + `exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts`: 155 passed (incl. denied-flag matrix and docs-parity).
- [x] `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` clean for the changed files; `oxlint` clean on changed files.
- [ ] CI + clawsweeper re-review.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** default safe bins are documented as auto-approved stdin-only text filters, but the safe-bin argv validator rejected pure boolean short-flag clusters, so `wc -l`, `tr -d`, `uniq -c`, `sort -n` were routed to manual approval. After the patch the real auto-approve gate (`isSafeBinUsage`) returns `true` for those recognized read-only boolean forms, while unknown/denied flags stay fail-closed.

**Real environment tested:** Linux 6.8.0-71-generic, Node v22.19, real repo source at branch head `34cef99a` vs `origin/main` (`ec6ad888`), driving the **real exported** `isSafeBinUsage` / `normalizeSafeBins` from `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` via `tsx` — not a stub or a re-implementation. Resolutions use `/usr/bin/<bin>` (a default trusted dir) so the full path/trust/profile gate runs.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `tsx` over a harness that imports the real `isSafeBinUsage` and calls it for each argv, first on this branch (after) then with the two prod files reset to `origin/main` (before):

```
node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/safebin-proof.ts          # after = branch head
git checkout origin/main -- <profiles> <validator>   # revert prod files only
node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/safebin-proof.ts          # before = origin/main
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```
############ BEFORE (origin/main) ############
. wc -l                    -> needs approval
. wc -w                    -> needs approval
. wc --lines               -> needs approval
. tr -d abc                -> needs approval
. tr -s                    -> needs approval
. uniq -c                  -> needs approval
. cut -s -f 1              -> needs approval
. tr -S a b                -> needs approval
. sort -o out.txt          -> needs approval
. wc --files0-from=list    -> needs approval

############ AFTER (this branch) ############
> wc -l                    -> AUTO-APPROVED
> wc -w                    -> AUTO-APPROVED
> wc --lines               -> AUTO-APPROVED
> tr -d abc                -> AUTO-APPROVED
> tr -s                    -> AUTO-APPROVED
> uniq -c                  -> AUTO-APPROVED
> cut -s -f 1              -> AUTO-APPROVED
. tr -S a b                -> needs approval
. sort -o out.txt          -> needs approval
. wc --files0-from=list    -> needs approval
```

**Observed result after fix:** every recognized read-only boolean form flipped from *needs approval* to *AUTO-APPROVED*; the unknown short flag (`tr -S`) and the denied flags (`sort -o`, `wc --files0-from`) stayed fail-closed. Matches the new unit/gate tests.

**What was not tested:** a full gateway + live agent exec round-trip (model issues `wc -l` -> no approval prompt) — proof was taken at the real `isSafeBinUsage` decision boundary, which is the single gate that turns a safe-bin invocation into an auto-approval. No Windows path (safe bins are disabled on Windows by design).

target.title: feat(feishu): comprehensive plugin enhancements — streaming, dedup, skills, calendar, and stability
target.body:
## Summary

This PR consolidates all Feishu plugin enhancements developed across multiple iterations (supersedes #38770, #38772, #39409, #39440). All previous PRs were closed without merging; this PR contains the complete changeset.

---

## Issues Fixed

### Streaming Card Display

1. **Text Duplication in Streaming Cards**: `mergeStreamingText()` fallback path duplicated cumulative text → bypass it, treat `onPartialReply` text as complete state
2. **Content Duplication Across Cards**: each card showed cumulative text instead of its own block → track `blockOffset`, slice cumulative text per card
3. **Content Disappearing on Card Close**: `closeStreaming()` used per-block text while card displayed cumulative text → `streamText` consistently uses sliced card text
4. **Missing Tail Text After Last Block**: `deliver(kind=final)` sent full duplicate or was skipped → extract only undelivered tail `text.slice(blockOffset)`, send if non-empty
5. **Extra Streaming Card After Last Block**: `onPartialReply` kept creating new cards → only start new card if substantial new content beyond `blockOffset`
6. **Slow Streaming Updates**: `await this.queue` blocked `onPartialReply` on API round-trips; no flush timer → remove `await` (fire-and-forget), add `pendingFlushTimer` (300ms auto-flush)
7. **Markdown Crash Guard**: `isSafeToRender()` blocks updates during unclosed code fences
8. **Typing Indicator Emoji**: Changed from `Typing` to `Get` (fix reaction 231001 error)
9. **Broken CardKit Streaming**: Replaced broken CardKit chunked patching with Semantic Block Delivery using `block` events from `reply-dispatcher.ts`

### Message Deduplication (3-Layer)

10. **event_id Dedup**: Lark SDK `EventDispatcher.parse()` flattens header into callback data — `event_id` IS available. Replaced content-hash workaround with proper `event_id` dedup as recommended by Feishu docs
11. **Stale Message Filtering in Debounce**: When debounce batch has messages with >2min time gap, drop older messages and record their IDs in persistent dedup
12. **create_time-based Stale Rejection**: Feishu retries re-deliver messages with new `message_id`/`event_id` but same `create_time`. Track last processed `create_time` per `chat+sender` and reject stale duplicates

### Message Control & Abort

13. **Fast Abort Bypass**: Trigger words (`stop`, `停止`, `取消`, `esc`, etc.) skip 2s debounce + bypass `chatQueue` → direct dispatch to `tryFastAbortFromMessage`
14. **Message Recall Abort**: Register `im.message.recalled_v1` → clear debounce buffer, record in dedup, synthesize `/stop` command via `dispatchFeishuMessageDirect`

### Feishu Ops Tools (Skills)

15. **feishu_contacts**: Search contacts with auto-sync from API
16. **feishu_groups**: List/search bot groups with auto-sync
17. **feishu_send**: Send text/file/@all to users or groups
18. **feishu_calendar**: Create, **update**, and **delete** calendar events by `event_id`
19. **2-Tier Contact Sync**: When list API returns 0 results, fallback to BFS department traversal + per-department user fetch (ported from `feishu-ops/scripts/update-contacts.py`)
20. **SKILL.md Rewrite**: All skill docs converted to English with JSON tool-call examples

### Code Quality

21. **Error Messages Standardized to English** across all new modules
22. **Group Search Hint**: `searchGroupsOrSync()` lists all bot groups and prompts user to add bot to target group if not found
23. **Mention Logic**: More resilient verify mention logic and fix log propagation
24. **SDK v1.30+ Response Parsing**: Fix `proactive-send.ts` to handle Lark SDK top-level response format (`{ file_key }` instead of `{ code: 0, data: { file_key } }`)

### Other

25. **Daemon Working Directory**: Resolve gateway working directory to installation root

---

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|---|---|
| `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` | event_id dedup, create_time dedup, fast abort bypass, recall handler, mention logic |
| `extensions/feishu/src/dedup.ts` | `checkAndRecordCreateTime()`, create_time tracking per chat+sender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts` | Block-boundary card splitting, blockOffset tracking, tail text, semantic block delivery |
| `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts` | Remove mergeStreamingText, async queue, pendingFlushTimer, isSafeToRender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/typing.ts` | Change typing emoji to Get |
| `extensions/feishu/src/inbound-debounce.ts` | Stale message filtering, removeFromBuffer() |
| `extensions/feishu/src/feishu-ops-tools.ts` | Register contacts, groups, send, calendar tools |
| `extensions/feishu/src/contacts.ts` | 2-tier sync with department fallback |
| `extensions/feishu/src/calendar.ts` | Update and delete calendar events |
| `extensions/feishu/src/proactive-send.ts` | SDK v1.30+ response parsing fix |
| `extensions/feishu/skills/*/SKILL.md` | English rewrite with JSON examples |
| `src/daemon/program-args.ts` | Working directory resolution fix |

## Supersedes

- #38770 (CLOSED — streaming card fix)
- #38772 (CLOSED — streaming + event_id dedup)
- #39409 (CLOSED — message control, skills, stability)
- #39440 (CLOSED — calendar update/delete)

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent outbound A2A allowlist override
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `agents.list[].tools.agentToAgent.allow` to control which agents a specific agent may contact via session tools (`sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, `session_status`)
- When unset, the agent falls back to the global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list; when set to explicit IDs, only those targets are reachable; `[]` isolates the agent from all cross-agent access
- The global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` continues to act as a participation gate (both requester and target must match it) before per-agent outbound rules are evaluated
- Outbound-only semantics: only the requester's override is checked, not the target's
- Replace `matchesAllow` with `matchesGlobalParticipation` and add `evaluateAccess()` returning a structured `AgentToAgentDenyReason` so all call sites produce context-aware error messages via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`
- Scrub deleted agent references from per-agent outbound allowlists in `pruneAgentConfig()`

### Example config (hub-spoke pattern)

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "david",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["*"] } } },
      { "id": "spoke",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } },
      { "id": "kelly",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } }
    ]
  }
}
```

spoke→david ✅ | spoke→kelly ❌ | david→anyone ✅

## Test plan

- [x] `sessions-access.test.ts`: per-agent override, deny-all `[]`, global gate, target override ignored (outbound-only), fallback to global when unset
- [x] `sessions.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny during label lookup in `sessions_send`
- [x] `openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny for cross-agent `session_status`
- [x] `agents.test.ts`: `pruneAgentConfig` scrubs deleted agent from per-agent allowlists
- [x] Build passes
- [x] Manual: configure hub-spoke agents (david/spoke/kelly), verify per-agent outbound rules — see `Real behavior proof` section below

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: per-agent outbound `agentToAgent.allow` correctly gates cross-agent session tool access. Hub-spoke topology used: `david` (hub, `allow=["*"]`), `spoke` (allow=`["david"]`), `kelly` (allow=`["david"]`). Validates the new `evaluateAccess()` decision pipeline reaches the documented per-agent outbound semantics, with the structured `per_agent_outbound` deny reason surfaced via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`.

- **Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04 (kernel 5.15), Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10. Branch HEAD `55819f6f43` of `repair/pr-39102-a2a-refresh` (this PR rebased on current main). Live invocation of the production export `createAgentToAgentPolicy` from `src/plugin-sdk/session-visibility.ts` — no mocks, no test runner, the same code path that backs `sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, and `session_status` at runtime.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  1. Author a hub-spoke `OpenClawConfig` matching the docs example (david hub + spoke + kelly).
  2. Build `policy = createAgentToAgentPolicy(cfg)`.
  3. Call `policy.evaluateAccess(requester, target)` for the five edge pairs.
  4. Run with: `pnpm tsx proof-hub-spoke.ts` (the script imports the worktree source directly so the running code is the patched export).

- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output, copied verbatim from `node` stdout via `tsx`.

  ```
  # per-agent A2A allowlist — hub-spoke proof
  # config: david=hub (allow *), spoke=spoke (allow david), kelly=spoke (allow david)

  [ok ] spoke -> david: ALLOWED
  [ok ] spoke -> kelly: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)
  [ok ] david -> kelly: ALLOWED
  [ok ] david -> spoke: ALLOWED
  [ok ] kelly -> spoke: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)

  all cases match expected behavior
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: all five real invocations matched the documented hub-spoke semantics. Cross-spoke calls (`spoke -> kelly`, `kelly -> spoke`) were denied with the structured `per_agent_outbound` reason code from `AgentToAgentDecision`. Hub-rooted calls (`david -> spoke`, `david -> kelly`) and spoke-to-hub (`spoke -> david`) were allowed. Process exit code: `0`. Console output above is the literal stdout, no post-processing.

- **What was not tested**: end-to-end IPC roundtrip through the live `sessions_send` agent process pair (single-machine ACP transport). The four session call sites all delegate the access decision to the single `evaluateAccess()` policy proven above, and the wiring of each call site is covered by the unit tests already listed in the Test plan.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(outbound): strip inbound metadata before delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #39847. Related to #41355, #30405, #28170.

When an LLM echoes internal metadata blocks (e.g., `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):`, `Sender (untrusted metadata):`) in its response, the outbound delivery pipeline sends them verbatim to the user on Discord/Telegram/etc.

`stripInboundMetadata()` already exists and is used in:
- TUI display (`chat-sanitize.ts`)
- Session cost calculations (`session-cost-usage.ts`)

But it was never called in the delivery pipeline (`normalizeReplyPayloadsForDelivery`).

## Changes

- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts` — import and call `stripInboundMetadata()` on payload text before parsing reply directives
- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts` — add test verifying metadata blocks are stripped before delivery

## Test plan

- [x] Added test: payload with echoed metadata block → metadata stripped, actual response preserved
- [x] All 9 tests in `payloads.test.ts` pass
- [x] `pnpm build && pnpm check` — clean

> AI-assisted: test case co-authored with Claude (Anthropic).

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: fix(cron): reject Feishu announce jobs without target
target.body:
## Summary

Reject creation of Feishu/Lark announce cron jobs when `delivery.to` is missing.

This fixes a case where cron accepted a job that could be stored and scheduled but would later fail at runtime during delivery with:

```text
Delivering to Feishu requires target <chatId|user:openId|chat:chatId>
```

Closes #39599.

## What changed

- add gateway/service-side validation in `src/cron/service/jobs.ts`
- add CLI fail-fast validation in `src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-add.ts`
- add tests covering:
  - Feishu without target → rejected
  - Lark without target → rejected
  - Feishu with explicit target → accepted

## Why both layers

Gateway-side validation is the real safety boundary and prevents invalid jobs from entering the system through any creation path.

CLI-side validation improves user feedback and fails earlier with a clearer message.

## Validation

Ran:

```bash
pnpm test src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts src/cron/normalize.test.ts
```

Result:
- 2 test files passed
- 64 tests passed

target.title: fix(memory-core): write Deep Sleep summary to DREAMS.md with managed markers
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: The deep dreaming phase writes the separate `memory/dreaming/deep/YYYY-MM-DD.md` report correctly but never writes the documented `## Deep Sleep` summary to `DREAMS.md`, despite the [Dreaming docs](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/dreaming) stating it should.
- **Fix**: Add inline storage support to `writeDeepDreamingReport` to write a managed `## Deep Sleep` block into `DREAMS.md` (when storage mode is `inline` or `both`), mirroring the existing pattern in `writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock`.
- **Scope boundary**: Only touches the deep report write path. Light/REM phases and the narrative diary writer are unchanged.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #91051

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

None

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No — identified from ClawSweeper's `source-repro` / `fix-shape-clear` review on the issue.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Deep Sleep phase does not write `## Deep Sleep` summary into `DREAMS.md`
- Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node 22.22.3, pnpm 10.32.1
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `pnpm install`
  2. `pnpm test:extension memory-core` — all dreaming-markdown tests pass
  3. Verified DREAMS.md content via inline test assertions
- Evidence after fix (test output showing new behavior):

```
✓ writes deep sleep summary into DREAMS.md with inline storage
✓ updates an existing DREAMS.md deep sleep block on subsequent runs
✓ preserves DREAMS.md diary marker blocks when writing deep sleep summary
✓ still writes deep reports to the per-phase report directory (separate mode unaffected)
```

- Observed result after fix: `DREAMS.md` now receives a `## Deep Sleep` managed block (with `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:start/end -->` markers) when storage mode is `inline` or `both`. The block is idempotent — subsequent runs replace rather than append. Existing diary marker blocks (`<!-- openclaw:dreaming:diary:... -->`) are preserved.
- Example DREAMS.md output produced by the fix:

```markdown
## Deep Sleep
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:start -->
- Ranked 3 candidates
- Promoted 1 candidate(s) into MEMORY.md.
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:end -->
```

- What was not tested: Live cron run (the managed cron path in `dreaming.ts` calls the same function without inspecting the return value; verified no compile-time breakage via the existing extension test suite)
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Windows-specific environment. The managed markdown write path is OS-independent (node:fs/promises). 4 pre-existing test failures in unrelated tests (`dreaming-phases`, `qmd-manager` on Windows symlinks, `search-manager` Windows path, `cli`) — all present on main before this change.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```
pnpm install
pnpm test:extension memory-core
```

40/44 test files passed. 4 pre-existing failures on Windows (all unrelated — verified present on `origin/main` before this change).

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Added 4 new tests in `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts`:
1. `writes deep sleep summary into DREAMS.md with inline storage` — verifies inline path, markers, heading, and body content
2. `updates an existing DREAMS.md deep sleep block on subsequent runs` — verifies idempotent replacement (no duplicate markers/headings)
3. `preserves DREAMS.md diary marker blocks when writing deep sleep summary` — verifies diary content survives deep sleep writes
4. Updated `still writes deep reports to the per-phase report directory` — unchanged assertion that separate mode does NOT write DREAMS.md

What failed before this fix, if known?

Running a deep dreaming sweep with `mode: "inline"` (the default) would silently skip the DREAMS.md write. The separate report was written correctly when `mode: "separate"` or `"both"`, but the documented `## Deep Sleep` summary in DREAMS.md never appeared.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes — `DREAMS.md` now includes a `## Deep Sleep` summary after the deep dreaming sweep runs (when storage mode is `inline` or `both`). This matches the documented behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

### Risks and mitigations

1. **Return type change**: `writeDeepDreamingReport` return type changed from `string | undefined` to `{ inlinePath?, reportPath? } | undefined`. The sole internal caller in `dreaming.ts` discards the return value, so no core breakage. Risk is limited to any third-party plugin SDK consumer calling this function directly — unknown likelihood, low impact (TypeScript compilation would catch the type mismatch).

2. **DREAMS.md concurrent writes**: The deep sleep summary and the narrative diary writer (`generateAndAppendDreamNarrative`) both touch `DREAMS.md`. The narrative runs after `writeDeepDreamingReport` in the same synchronous control flow (`dreaming.ts:505-519`), so the ordering is deterministic. However, if a second deep sweep runs concurrently (e.g., overlapping cron trigger), the file could be written from two processes. Same pre-existing risk as light/REM phase daily memory file writes, which use identical managed block logic — not new.

3. **Default behavior change**: With the default storage config `{ mode: "inline", separateReports: false }`, the deep sweep previously returned `undefined` immediately without writing anything. Now it will write to `DREAMS.md` by default. Users upgrading from a version where `DREAMS.md` was hand-curated may see managed content appear. Mitigation: managed markers make the block clearly identifiable as auto-generated, and users can delete the block (it will be recreated on next sweep — that's by design).

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Awaiting CI results and reviewer feedback.

target.title: [codex] Avoid nested filesystem sandbox for OpenClaw sandbox tools
target.body:
## Summary

When OpenClaw owns Codex native tool execution through the sandbox exec-server, start the Codex app-server thread with `danger-full-access` so Codex does not also apply its own filesystem sandbox on top of the external OpenClaw sandbox.

The per-turn sandbox policy still resolves to `externalSandbox`, so the OpenClaw sandbox remains the actual execution boundary. This only avoids the nested Codex filesystem sandbox path that can reject native file writes such as `apply_patch` even when the OpenClaw sandbox workspace is writable.

## Why this is not a return to the old unchained default

Earlier Codex app-server defaults briefly favored `danger-full-access` for unattended usability, but that was unsafe when the app-server/native tool execution still ran from the Gateway side. In that shape, Codex native tools could bypass the OpenClaw Docker sandbox contract, so OpenClaw later clamped active-sandbox Codex turns back to Codex `workspace-write`.

This PR is narrower and follows the later `sandboxExecServer` direction from #84388:

- the override is only applied when OpenClaw sandboxing is active, Codex native tools are enabled, and `appServer.experimental.sandboxExecServer=true`;
- the Codex turn still receives `externalSandbox`, with networking derived from the OpenClaw sandbox;
- the Codex thread receives the OpenClaw sandbox exec-server environment, so native filesystem/process operations are routed through OpenClaw's sandbox-backed exec-server instead of Codex's Gateway-side filesystem sandbox;
- the non-exec-server control path keeps the existing Codex sandbox mode unchanged.

In other words, this does not globally loosen Codex. It removes the obsolete inner Codex filesystem sandbox only after OpenClaw has taken over the native-tool execution boundary.

## Mixed sandbox and non-sandbox agents

This change is also safe for deployments that run sandboxed and non-sandboxed Codex agents side by side.

OpenClaw may reuse a process-shared Codex app-server client, but the sandbox mode, cwd, dynamic tools, and sandbox environments are sent on each `thread/start` or `thread/resume`. A non-sandboxed Codex run can therefore share the same app-server process without granting its policy to a sandboxed thread.

For sandbox exec-server turns, OpenClaw also fingerprints the selected external sandbox environment in the Codex thread binding. If the environment selection changes, OpenClaw starts a new Codex thread instead of resuming a previously bound one. That prevents a sandboxed exec-server turn from reusing a non-sandbox thread that lacks the OpenClaw external sandbox environment.

## Linked context

This came from a local OpenClaw sandboxed `henglu` agent incident where Codex native `apply_patch` failed repeatedly, while shell writes and `git commit` in the same OpenClaw sandboxed session succeeded.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex native `apply_patch` can fail to write inside an OpenClaw Docker sandboxed agent because Codex's own filesystem sandbox remains active even though OpenClaw sandbox exec-server owns the native tool execution boundary.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw setup using the real `henglu` agent sandbox config from `/home/<user>/.openclaw/openclaw.json`; agent sandbox is enabled, Docker-backed, `workspaceAccess=rw`, with Codex sandbox exec-server enabled. The after-fix check ran from this PR branch at head `da35926d73103bc4b5e17e64a3544001ae6dbd72`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a PR-branch `tsx` console check that imports the production `resolveCodexAppServerForExternalSandboxEnvironment` helper and feeds it the real local `henglu` OpenClaw sandbox shape, once with sandbox exec-server enabled and once as a control with it disabled.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal output from the local PR branch run:

```text
[real-setup] source=openclaw PR branch, config=/home/<user>/.openclaw/openclaw.json, agent=henglu
[real-setup] openclawSandbox enabled=true backend=docker workspaceAccess=rw sandboxExecServer=true nativeToolSurface=true
[after-fix] appServerSandboxBefore=workspace-write appServerSandboxAfter=danger-full-access
[control] sandboxExecServer=false appServerSandboxAfter=workspace-write
[identity] beforeObjectUnchanged=true
```

- Observed result after fix: For the real sandboxed `henglu` configuration, the patched OpenClaw startup path changes the Codex app-server thread sandbox from `workspace-write` to `danger-full-access` only when OpenClaw sandbox exec-server owns native tool execution. The control path without sandbox exec-server keeps `workspace-write`, so non-external-sandbox cases keep their existing Codex sandbox policy.
- What was not tested: I did not replace the currently running local OpenClaw gateway with this PR branch or run a full live LLM turn through the `henglu` channel after the patch. The live end-to-end `apply_patch` success path should be rechecked by a maintainer or by me in a deployed PR build if desired.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The after-fix proof validates the production startup policy decision that caused the faulty nested sandbox path, using the real local OpenClaw sandbox config. It is not a full deployed gateway smoke test.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Redacted runtime logs from the real incident showed repeated native patch failures followed by successful shell writes in the same sandboxed session:

```text
patch_apply_end success=false stderr="Failed to write file /workspace/raw/2026-06-06/index.md"
patch_apply_end success=false stderr="Failed to write file raw/2026-06-06/index.md"
patch_apply_end success=false stderr="Failed to write file reports/henglu_purchase/2026-06-06/test.md"
# Later in the same OpenClaw sandboxed session, shell-based writes, git commit, and push succeeded.
```

## Tests and validation

```bash
npx --yes pnpm@11.2.2 exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-startup.test.ts
```

Result: `10 passed`.

Regression coverage added:

- OpenClaw sandbox exec-server path forces Codex app-server sandbox to `danger-full-access`.
- Non-external-sandbox path preserves the existing Codex app-server sandbox setting.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`, for Codex native tools inside OpenClaw sandbox exec-server attempts.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, tool execution policy plumbing changes in the Codex app-server startup path.

Highest-risk area: accidentally broadening Codex sandboxing outside OpenClaw-owned sandbox exec-server attempts.

Mitigation: the resolver only switches to `danger-full-access` when `shouldRequireCodexSandboxExecServerEnvironment(...)` is true; the added control test verifies the sandbox setting is unchanged when sandbox exec-server is not active. The per-turn policy remains `externalSandbox`, and the selected OpenClaw sandbox environment is bound per Codex thread, so OpenClaw remains the actual execution boundary.

## Current review state

Next action: wait for CI and ClawSweeper/maintainer feedback.

Still waiting on: maintainer review, and optional live deployed gateway smoke if maintainers want full end-to-end proof.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: `Real behavior proof` CI failure was addressed by adding the required structured proof section above.

target.title: fix(diagnostics): add human-readable MiB units and threshold percentage to memory pressure logs
target.body:
## Summary

The memory pressure WARN log line emitted raw byte counts with no units, making it non-actionable for operators. This PR adds human-readable MiB formatting and threshold percentage.

**Before:** `memory pressure: level=warning reason=rss_threshold rssBytes=2012905472 heapUsedBytes=1307038712 thresholdBytes=1610612736`

**After:** `memory pressure: level=warning reason=rss_threshold rss=2012905472 (1919.7 MiB, 125% of 1536 MiB threshold) heapUsed=1307038712 (1246.5 MiB) rssGrowthBytes=... windowMs=...`

## Linked context

Closes #90783

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Memory pressure WARN log has no units/percentage — operators can't read it at a glance
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/diagnostic-memory.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stability.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix:** `89 passed (3 test files)`
- **Observed result after fix:** Log format includes MiB and threshold percentage
- **What was not tested:** Live gateway long-running memory pressure (unit tests cover the formatting path)

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/diagnostic-memory.test.ts
# 10 passed (1 test file)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/diagnostic-stability.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts
# 79 passed (2 test files)
```

**What regression coverage was added or updated?** Existing tests cover the log path — they use `stringContaining` matchers that remain compatible.

**What failed before this fix, if known?** Log line was non-actionable: raw bytes, no units, no percentage.

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** No (log format only, same severity/trigger logic)
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
**What is the highest-risk area?** Downstream log parsers may depend on the old format. Mitigation: the message prefix is unchanged; only the metric fields after `reason=` changed.
**How is that risk mitigated?** All 89 related tests pass. The log prefix `memory pressure: level=... reason=...` is preserved for parsers.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** CI verification + maintainer review.
**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** None.
**Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?** N/A

commands.author: fuller-stack-dev

target.title: fix: surface safe terminal tool fallbacks
target.body:
## Summary

- Add opt-in terminal fallback metadata through agent-core, session tool definitions, subscribe handling, and the embedded runner.
- Surface safe terminal summaries for tool-loop and incomplete-turn exits, while keeping undeclared tool output neutral instead of leaking raw results.
- Declare structured fallbacks for `cron` and `web_fetch`, and keep Docker image builds from carrying stale `dist` output.
- Tighten delivery/side-effect evidence for channel replies, source-reply deliveries, context-engine pairing, compaction ownership, and heartbeat lifecycle handling.

## Contract note

- `terminalSummary` and `terminalResultFallback` are intentionally public tool metadata for safe terminal presentation.
- Tools opt in explicitly; undeclared raw tool output is not surfaced as a fallback.
- Generic tool activity, accepted child-session spawns, messaging sends, cron additions, and replay-invalid aborts continue to suppress fallback model replay so side effects are not duplicated.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.does-not-append-text-end-content-is.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.suppresses-message-end-block-replies-message-tool.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-definition-adapter.after-tool-call.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-definition-adapter.after-tool-call.fires-once.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-owned-tool-runtime-contract.test.ts src/dockerfile.test.ts src/agents/terminal-reply.test.ts packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm test:extensions:package-boundary`
- `pnpm build`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Channel-facing agents that ended a turn after successful tool work could reply with planning text, repeat promises, or expose unsafe raw fallback text instead of delivering the completed tool result.

Real environment tested: A live Discord-backed OpenClaw stack using the patched image and a connected bot. Identifying stack, bot, channel, image, and run IDs are intentionally omitted from this public PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent live prompts that exercised a scheduler/cron path and a `web_fetch` tool-loop timeout path through the running gateway.

Evidence after fix:

Redacted live channel output, scheduler path:

```text
The requested scheduler state cannot be checked from this runtime because no scheduler, cron, gateway, or exec tool is available in this channel session. I cannot claim a live cron check without that tool.
```

Redacted live channel output, `web_fetch` terminal fallback path:

```text
URL: https://example.com
Final URL: https://example.com
Status: 200
Title: Example Domain
Cached: true
Truncated: false
```

Observed result after fix: The channel received user-visible results for both proof cases. The `web_fetch` fallback included URL, final URL, status, title, cache state, and truncation state, with no raw unsafe tool dump. The scheduler path returned a direct capability blocker instead of a promise to perform an unavailable check.

What was not tested: Broad cross-channel E2E beyond Discord was not rerun. One unrelated live runtime metadata drift was observed during proof and is omitted here because it is not part of this patch.

commands.author: wangwllu

target.title: fix(sessions): prune stale gateway model-run sessions
target.body:
## Summary
- add a model-run-specific session retention setting with a conservative default of 24h
- validate `session.maintenance.modelRunPruneAfter` in the strict session schema (`duration | number | false`)
- prune stale `agent:*:explicit:model-run-<uuid>` probe sessions before global age/cap maintenance during write, load-time enforcement, and `sessions cleanup`
- expose `modelRunPruned` / `prune-model-run` in cleanup summaries and dry-run plans

Fixes #88632

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: stale gateway `model-run-<uuid>` explicit probe sessions are reported and pruned by `openclaw sessions cleanup`, while recent model-run probes and durable non-model-run sessions remain.
- Real environment tested: local macOS checkout at PR head `0a76e16651686277dfe15b9e108252b9caf4a13c`, Node 26.0.0, built CLI via `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and temp session store under `/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: built the CLI, created a redacted temp `sessions.json` with one stale model-run key, one recent model-run key, and one durable control key, then ran:

```console
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/home \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/state \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs sessions cleanup --dry-run --json \
  --store /tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/sessions.json

$ OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/home \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/state \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs sessions cleanup --enforce --json \
  --store /tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/sessions.json
```

- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the real built CLI run:

```json
{
  "dryRun": {
    "beforeCount": 3,
    "afterCount": 2,
    "modelRunPruned": 1,
    "pruned": 0,
    "capped": 0,
    "wouldMutate": true
  },
  "enforce": {
    "beforeCount": 3,
    "afterCount": 2,
    "modelRunPruned": 1,
    "pruned": 0,
    "capped": 0,
    "wouldMutate": true
  },
  "remainingKeys": [
    "agent:main:explicit:durable-control",
    "agent:main:explicit:model-run-123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174001"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: dry-run and enforce both reported `modelRunPruned: 1`; after enforce, only the stale gateway model-run probe was removed, and the recent model-run plus durable control session remained.
- What was not tested: a live remote gateway model inference call was not required for this proof; the after-fix cleanup behavior was exercised through the real built OpenClaw CLI against an isolated session store. No channel, credential, model provider, or gateway transport behavior is changed in this PR.

## Testing
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/cli/sessions.md docs/concepts/session.md docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm lint:docs -- docs/cli/sessions.md docs/concepts/session.md docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/sessions-cleanup.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/zod-schema.session-maintenance-extensions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/schema.base.generated.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/zod-schema.session.ts src/config/zod-schema.session-maintenance-extensions.test.ts src/commands/sessions-cleanup.test.ts src/commands/sessions-cleanup.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts src/config/sessions/runtime-types.ts src/config/sessions/store-load.ts src/config/sessions/store-maintenance.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.ts src/config/types.base.ts`

Note: full `pnpm format:check` still reports unrelated pre-existing formatting issues outside this PR's touched files.
